mappings.evidence_note: Title page states 'AP Physics 1: Algebra-Based Course and Exam Description, Effective Fall 2024'.
AP Physics 1 is an algebra-based, introductory college-level physics course; 8 content areas listed; prerequisites Geometry + concurrent Algebra II; 25% instructional time in hands-on lab work.

Three science practices: Practice 1 Creating Representations (skills 1.A, 1.B, 1.C), Practice 2 Mathematical Routines (skills 2.A, 2.B, 2.C, 2.D), Practice 3 Scientific Questioning and Argumentation (skills 3.A, 3.B, 3.C).

8 units with multiple-choice exam weightings: Unit 1 Kinematics 10-15%, Unit 2 Force and Translational Dynamics 18-23%, Unit 3 Work/Energy/Power 18-23%, Unit 4 Linear Momentum 10-15%, Unit 5 Torque and Rotational Dynamics 10-15%, Unit 6 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems 5-8%, Unit 7 Oscillations 5-8%, Unit 8 Fluids 10-15%.

Unit 1 Kinematics topics: 1.1 Scalars and Vectors in One Dimension, 1.2 Displacement Velocity and Acceleration, 1.3 Representing Motion, 1.4 Reference Frames and Relative Motion, 1.5 Vectors and Motion in Two Dimensions.

Unit 2 Force and Translational Dynamics topics: 2.1 Systems and Center of Mass, 2.2 Forces and Free-Body Diagrams, 2.3 Newton's Third Law, 2.4 Newton's First Law, 2.5 Newton's Second Law, 2.6 Gravitational Force, 2.7 Kinetic and Static Friction, 2.8 Spring Forces, 2.9 Circular Motion.
Unit 3 Work, Energy, and Power topics: 3.1 Translational Kinetic Energy, 3.2 Work, 3.3 Potential Energy, 3.4 Conservation of Energy, 3.5 Power.
Unit 4 Linear Momentum topics: 4.1 Linear Momentum, 4.2 Change in Momentum and Impulse, 4.3 Conservation of Linear Momentum, 4.4 Elastic and Inelastic Collisions.

Unit 5 Torque and Rotational Dynamics topics: 5.1 Rotational Kinematics, 5.2 Connecting Linear and Rotational Motion, 5.3 Torque, 5.4 Rotational Inertia, 5.5 Rotational Equilibrium and Newton's First Law in Rotational Form, 5.6 Newton's Second Law in Rotational Form.

Unit 6 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems topics: 6.1 Rotational Kinetic Energy, 6.2 Torque and Work, 6.3 Angular Momentum and Angular Impulse, 6.4 Conservation of Angular Momentum, 6.5 Rolling, 6.6 Motion of Orbiting Satellites.
Unit 7 Oscillations topics: 7.1 Defining Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), 7.2 Frequency and Period of SHM, 7.3 Representing and Analyzing SHM, 7.4 Energy of Simple Harmonic Oscillators.
Unit 8 Fluids topics: 8.1 Internal Structure and Density, 8.2 Pressure, 8.3 Fluids and Newton's Laws, 8.4 Fluids and Conservation Laws.
Section I: 40 multiple-choice questions, 50% weighting, 80 minutes.

Section II: 4 free-response questions, 50% weighting, 100 minutes. Question types: Mathematical Routines, Translation Between Representations, Experimental Design and Analysis, Qualitative/Quantitative Translation.
Approximate MCQ and FRQ weightings by skill (1.A, 1.B, 1.C, 2.A, 2.B, 2.C, 2.D, 3.A, 3.B, 3.C).

FRQ question styles: Mathematical Routines (10 pts, 20-25 min), Translation Between Representations (12 pts, 25-30 min), Experimental Design and Analysis (10 pts, 25-30 min), Qualitative/Quantitative Translation (8 pts, 15-20 min).
Task verbs used in free-response questions: Calculate, Compare, Derive, Describe, Determine, Draw, Estimate, Indicate, Justify, Label, Plot, Rank, Sketch, Verify.
25% of instructional time must be spent in hands-on laboratory work with inquiry-based investigations.
Table of Information: Equations in Appendix.
Vocabulary and Definitions of Important Ideas in AP Physics.
